cTreeBalls
  • Python interface
  • View page source

Python interface

The Python extension is implemented by python/cballys.pyx and declared to Cython through python/ccballys.pxd. It wraps the same C lifecycle used by the cballs executable, but exposes it as a Python class named cballs.

Basic lifecycle

Typical Python use follows this pattern:

from cballys import cballs

cb = cballs()
cb.set(
    infile="catalog.txt",
    infileformat="columns-ascii",
    rootDir="Output",
    options="compute-HistN",
)
cb.Run()
rbins = cb.getrBins()
xi2 = cb.getHistXi2pcf()

set updates the internal parameter dictionary. Run converts that dictionary into the C file_content representation, calls the startup routines, executes the main search, and leaves C-owned histograms available for getter methods. clean_all releases C allocations and clears the Python parameter dictionary.

Run levels

Run(level=[...]) accepts the name of the last C stage the caller needs. The wrapper expands dependencies automatically. The default level is ["MainLoop"], which performs a full calculation. Internally the common stage order is:

  1. input reads Python parameters into C structures.

  2. StartRun_Common validates parameters, initializes state, loads input catalogs or test data, and allocates memory.

  3. PrintParameterFile writes a reproducible parameter snapshot.

  4. SetNumberThreads applies the OpenMP thread count.

  5. MainLoop builds trees, runs the selected search, and evaluates histograms.

Available getters

The wrapper exposes small, NumPy-oriented getters for the C arrays used most often from notebooks and scripts:

getrBins

Radial bin centers or edges as prepared by the C run.

getHistNN and getHistCF

Neighbor counts and correlation-function outputs.

getHistXi2pcf

Two-point correlation function array.

getHistZetaMsincos(m, type) and getHistZetaM_EE(m)

Two-dimensional 3PCF/multipole matrices for the requested multipole index.

getNThreads, getTheta, getrsmooth, getCPUTime, getVersion, getRootDir, and allocation-flag getters expose selected scalar state from the underlying C structs.

Cython interface

python/ccballys.pxd intentionally declares only the fields and C functions needed by the wrapper. When a C field is renamed, moved, or newly needed by Python, update the .pxd declaration and the matching logic in python/cballys.pyx together.

Memory is owned by the C code after StartRun_Common. The wrapper tracks C allocation flags and calls the granular EndRun_FreeMemory_* routines from struct_cleanup. New C allocations that should survive long enough for Python getters must also be reachable by the cleanup path.

See also

For a broader map of the C modules and Python wrapper, see Code structure.
